u1_l1.1.title: Layers of the Earth (Crust, Mantle, Core)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Layers of the Earth (Crust, Mantle, Core)
summary_sections.content_html:
Earth's Internal Structure

Layers by Composition

  • Crust: Thin outermost layer. Continental crust (25-70 km, granitic) and oceanic crust (5-10 km, basaltic).
  • Mantle: Thick layer of silicate rock (2,900 km). Upper mantle contains the asthenosphere (partially molten).
  • Core: Iron-nickel. Outer core is liquid (2,200 km), inner core is solid (1,220 km radius) due to immense pressure.

Layers by Physical Properties

  • Lithosphere: Rigid crust + uppermost mantle; broken into tectonic plates.
  • Asthenosphere: Partially molten, flows slowly; plates glide over it.
  • Mesosphere: Lower mantle, stronger but still capable of slow flow.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Minerals (Classification, Identification Tests)
summary_sections.content_html:
Minerals

What Defines a Mineral?

  • Naturally occurring, inorganic, solid, definite chemical composition, and crystalline structure.

Identification Tests

  • Hardness: Mohs scale (1-talc to 10-diamond).
  • Luster: Metallic vs. non-metallic (vitreous, pearly, earthy).
  • Streak: Color of powdered mineral on unglazed porcelain.
  • Cleavage/Fracture: How mineral breaks — along flat planes (cleavage) or irregular surfaces (fracture).
  • Specific Gravity: Density relative to water.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Rock Cycle (Igneous, Sedimentary, Metamorphic)
summary_sections.content_html:
The Rock Cycle

Three Rock Types

  • Igneous: From cooled magma/lava. Intrusive (slow cooling, large crystals) vs. extrusive (fast cooling, small/no crystals).
  • Sedimentary: From compacted/cemented sediments. Clastic (broken pieces), chemical (precipitation), organic (biological remains).
  • Metamorphic: From existing rock changed by heat and/or pressure. Foliated (banded) vs. non-foliated.

Cycle Processes

  • Weathering → Erosion → Deposition → Compaction/Cementation → Uplift → Metamorphism → Melting → Crystallization

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Plate Tectonics (Continental Drift, Seafloor Spreading)
summary_sections.content_html:
Plate Tectonics

Evidence for Continental Drift

  • Puzzle-fit of coastlines (Africa & South America).
  • Matching fossils across oceans (Mesosaurus, Glossopteris).
  • Glacial striations in tropical regions.

Seafloor Spreading

  • Mid-ocean ridges produce new oceanic crust.
  • Magnetic striping: symmetric reversals on either side of ridge.
  • Age of ocean floor increases with distance from ridge.

Plate Boundaries

  • Divergent: Plates move apart → new crust (mid-ocean ridges, rift valleys).
  • Convergent: Plates collide → subduction, mountains, or volcanic arcs.
  • Transform: Plates slide past each other → earthquakes (San Andreas Fault).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Earthquakes (Seismic Waves, Measurement, Prediction)
summary_sections.content_html:
Earthquakes

Causes

Sudden release of energy from rock fracturing along faults. Most occur at plate boundaries.

Seismic Waves

  • P-waves (Primary): Fastest; compressional; travel through solids, liquids, and gases.
  • S-waves (Secondary): Slower; shear; travel only through solids.
  • Surface waves: Slowest but most destructive; Rayleigh and Love waves.

Measurement

  • Moment Magnitude Scale: Measures total energy released. Each whole number = ~32× more energy.
  • Seismograph: Instrument that records ground motion.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Volcanoes (Types, Eruptions, Hazards)
summary_sections.content_html:
Volcanoes

Types of Volcanoes

  • Shield: Broad, gently sloping; low-viscosity basaltic lava (Mauna Loa, Hawaii).
  • Cinder Cone: Smallest; steep sides; explosive fragments (Parícutin).
  • Composite (Stratovolcano): Tall, steep; alternating lava/ash layers; explosive (Mt. St. Helens, Fuji).

Eruption Factors

  • Viscosity: Higher silica = more viscous magma = more explosive.
  • Gas Content: More dissolved gas = more explosive eruptions.
  • Temperature: Hotter magma is less viscous, flows more easily.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mountain Building & Geological Structures
summary_sections.content_html:
Mountain Building (Orogeny)

Types of Mountains

  • Fold Mountains: Formed by compression at convergent boundaries (Himalayas, Appalachians).
  • Fault-Block Mountains: Formed by tensional forces lifting blocks along normal faults (Sierra Nevada, Tetons).
  • Volcanic Mountains: Built by eruptions (Mt. Rainier, Mt. Fuji).
  • Dome Mountains: Uplifted by magma pushing up without erupting (Black Hills).

Geological Structures

  • Anticline: Upward fold (arch). Syncline: Downward fold (trough).
  • Fault types: Normal (tension), reverse/thrust (compression), strike-slip (shear).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Geological Resources (Ores, Fossil Fuels)
summary_sections.content_html:
Geological Resources

Metallic Ores

  • Formed by magmatic, hydrothermal, sedimentary, and weathering processes.
  • Examples: iron (hematite), copper (chalcopyrite), gold (native), aluminum (bauxite).

Fossil Fuels

  • Coal: From ancient swamp vegetation; peat → lignite → bituminous → anthracite.
  • Oil & Natural Gas: From marine organisms buried under sediment; require source rock, migration, and trap.

Non-Metallic Resources

  • Sand, gravel, limestone (cement), gypsum (drywall), clay, phosphates (fertilizer).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Natural Hazards
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Natural Hazards

Earthquake Case Studies

  • 2011 Tōhoku, Japan (M9.1): Triggered massive tsunami; Fukushima nuclear disaster; ~20,000 deaths.
  • 2010 Haiti (M7.0): Devastating due to poor construction; ~200,000+ deaths; highlighted vulnerability.

Volcanic Case Studies

  • 1980 Mt. St. Helens: Lateral blast; 57 deaths; ecosystem recovery studied.
  • 79 AD Pompeii (Vesuvius): Pyroclastic flows buried city; preserved in ash.

Lessons Learned

  • Building codes, early warning systems, community preparedness, and land-use planning save lives.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Composition & Layers of the Atmosphere
summary_sections.content_html:
Atmospheric Composition & Structure

Composition

  • Nitrogen 78%, Oxygen 21%, Argon 0.93%, CO₂ 0.04%, trace gases and water vapor.

Atmospheric Layers

  • Troposphere (0-12 km): Weather occurs here; temperature decreases with altitude.
  • Stratosphere (12-50 km): Contains ozone layer; temperature increases with altitude.
  • Mesosphere (50-85 km): Coldest layer; meteors burn up here.
  • Thermosphere (85-600 km): Very hot but thin; auroras occur here.
  • Exosphere (600+ km): Transitions into space.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Energy Transfer (Radiation, Conduction, Convection)
summary_sections.content_html:
Energy Transfer in Earth's Atmosphere

Three Methods

  • Radiation: Transfer by electromagnetic waves; how the Sun heats Earth (no medium required).
  • Conduction: Direct contact transfer; Earth's surface heats air touching it.
  • Convection: Transfer by fluid movement; warm air rises, cool air sinks → convection cells.

Earth's Energy Budget

  • ~30% of solar radiation reflected (albedo), ~70% absorbed by surface and atmosphere.
  • Earth radiates infrared back; greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit some, warming the surface.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Weather Systems (Fronts, Pressure Systems)
summary_sections.content_html:
Weather Systems

Air Masses & Fronts

  • Air masses: Large bodies of air with uniform T and humidity (Continental/Maritime × Tropical/Polar/Arctic).
  • Cold front: Cold air pushes under warm air → steep boundary, heavy rain, then clearing.
  • Warm front: Warm air slides over cold air → gradual boundary, steady precipitation.
  • Stationary front: Neither air mass advances; prolonged cloudiness/rain.
  • Occluded front: Cold front catches warm front; complex weather.

Pressure Systems

  • Low pressure (cyclone): Rising air, convergence, clouds, precipitation.
  • High pressure (anticyclone): Sinking air, divergence, clear skies.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Clouds & Precipitation Types
summary_sections.content_html:
Clouds & Precipitation

Cloud Classification

  • Cirrus: High, thin, wispy. Made of ice crystals.
  • Stratus: Low, flat, layered. Steady drizzle or overcast.
  • Cumulus: Puffy, vertical development. Fair weather or thunderstorms (cumulonimbus).
  • Nimbus prefix/suffix: Rain-bearing (nimbostratus, cumulonimbus).

Precipitation Formation

  • Bergeron process: Ice crystals grow at the expense of supercooled water droplets in mixed clouds.
  • Collision-coalescence: Droplets collide and merge (warm tropical clouds).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Climate Zones & Global Circulation
summary_sections.content_html:
Climate Zones & Global Circulation

Global Wind Belts

  • Trade Winds: 0-30° latitude; blow NE in NH, SE in SH.
  • Westerlies: 30-60°; prevailing winds from west to east.
  • Polar Easterlies: 60-90°; cold, dry winds from the poles.

Climate Classification (Köppen)

  • A (Tropical): Warm year-round, high precipitation.
  • B (Dry/Arid): Evaporation exceeds precipitation.
  • C (Temperate): Moderate; mild winters.
  • D (Continental): Large temperature range; cold winters.
  • E (Polar): Cold year-round.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Severe Weather (Hurricanes, Tornadoes, Blizzards)
summary_sections.content_html:
Severe Weather

Hurricanes (Tropical Cyclones)

  • Form over warm ocean water (≥26.5°C). Fueled by latent heat from condensation.
  • Saffir-Simpson scale: Cat 1 (119 km/h) to Cat 5 (252+ km/h).

Tornadoes

  • Violently rotating column of air from a thunderstorm to the ground.
  • Enhanced Fujita (EF) scale: EF0-EF5 based on damage.

Blizzards

  • Sustained winds ≥56 km/h with heavy snow (or blowing snow) reducing visibility to <400 m for ≥3 hours.

u2_l2.7.title: Human Impact on Atmosphere (Pollution, Greenhouse Gases)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Human Impact on Atmosphere (Pollution, Greenhouse Gases)
summary_sections.content_html:
Human Impact on the Atmosphere

Air Pollution

  • Primary pollutants: Emitted directly (CO, SO₂, NOx, particulates, VOCs).
  • Secondary pollutants: Formed by reactions (ozone, smog, acid rain).

Enhanced Greenhouse Effect

  • Burning fossil fuels increases CO₂ (from ~280 ppm pre-industrial to 420+ ppm today).
  • Other greenhouse gases: methane (CH₄), nitrous oxide (N₂O), fluorinated gases.
  • Global average temperature has risen ~1.1°C since pre-industrial times.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Weather Prediction Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Weather Prediction

Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP)

  • Computer models solve atmospheric equations using current observations as initial conditions.
  • Models like GFS, ECMWF, NAM use different resolutions and physics.

Data Sources

  • Surface stations, weather balloons (radiosondes), satellites, radar, aircraft, ocean buoys.

Forecast Accuracy

  • 3-day forecasts now are as accurate as 1-day forecasts were 30 years ago.
  • Accuracy decreases significantly beyond ~10 days (chaos theory).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Climate Events
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Climate Events

El Niño / La Niña (ENSO)

El Niño: warm Pacific → increased US winter rainfall, droughts in Australia. La Niña: cool Pacific → more Atlantic hurricanes, flooding in SE Asia.

Drought & Heat Events

2012 US drought (worst since 1950s). European heat wave 2003 (~70,000 excess deaths). Rising frequency linked to climate change.

Polar Vortex Events

Weakened polar vortex allows Arctic air to plunge into mid-latitudes, causing extreme cold events (e.g., February 2021 Texas freeze).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Hydrologic Cycle (Evaporation, Condensation, Precipitation)
summary_sections.content_html:
The Hydrologic (Water) Cycle

Key Processes

  • Evaporation: Liquid water → water vapor (solar energy driven). Transpiration adds plant water.
  • Condensation: Water vapor → liquid droplets (forms clouds when air cools to dew point).
  • Precipitation: Water falls as rain, snow, sleet, or hail when cloud droplets grow large enough.
  • Infiltration: Water soaks into soil and rock, recharging groundwater.
  • Runoff: Water flows over the surface into streams, rivers, lakes, and oceans.

Water Budget

Earth's water is constant — the cycle redistributes it. ~97.5% salt water, ~2.5% fresh (most locked in ice).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Oceans (Currents, Salinity, Temperature Layers)
summary_sections.content_html:
Ocean Properties

Salinity

  • Average ocean salinity ~35 ppt (parts per thousand). Higher near evaporative regions, lower near river mouths and rain.

Temperature Layers

  • Surface (mixed) layer: Warm, well-mixed by waves and wind (0-200 m).
  • Thermocline: Rapid temperature decrease with depth (200-1,000 m).
  • Deep ocean: Cold and nearly uniform (~1-4°C) below 1,000 m.

Currents

  • Surface currents: Wind-driven; form gyres (clockwise NH, counterclockwise SH).
  • Deep currents: Thermohaline circulation driven by density differences (temperature + salinity).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Waves & Tides (Causes, Effects)
summary_sections.content_html:
Waves & Tides

Waves

  • Generated primarily by wind. Wave height depends on wind speed, duration, and fetch.
  • Waves transport energy, not water — water moves in circular orbits.
  • Waves break when depth becomes less than half the wavelength.

Tides

  • Caused by: Gravitational pull of the Moon (primary) and Sun.
  • Spring tides: Sun and Moon aligned → highest highs, lowest lows.
  • Neap tides: Sun and Moon at 90° → smaller tidal range.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Freshwater Systems (Rivers, Lakes, Aquifers)
summary_sections.content_html:
Freshwater Systems

Rivers & Streams

  • Drainage networks from headwaters to mouth. Shaped by gradient, discharge, and load.
  • Meanders form on gently sloping terrain; V-shaped valleys in steep terrain.

Lakes

  • Formed by glaciers, tectonics, volcanic activity, or river/dam processes.
  • Stratified in summer: warm epilimnion, thermocline, cold hypolimnion.

Groundwater

  • Stored in aquifers — porous rock below the water table. Ogallala Aquifer supplies ~30% of US irrigation.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Glaciers & Ice Caps (Formation, Melting Impacts)
summary_sections.content_html:
Glaciers & Ice Caps

Formation & Types

  • Glaciers form when snow accumulates faster than it melts, compressing into ice over years.
  • Alpine (valley): Flow down mountain valleys.
  • Continental (ice sheets): Cover vast areas (Greenland, Antarctica).

Glacial Features

  • Erosion: cirques, U-shaped valleys, horns, arêtes.
  • Deposition: moraines, drumlins, erratics, outwash plains.

Climate Implications

  • If all ice melted, sea level would rise ~65-70 meters. Current rate: ~3.5 mm/year sea level rise.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Water Resources & Human Use
summary_sections.content_html:
Water Resources & Human Use

Water Use

  • Agriculture (~70% global), Industry (~20%), Domestic (~10%).
  • Water stress: using >25% of available renewable supply.

Water Management

  • Dams and reservoirs for storage and flood control.
  • Desalination converting salt water to fresh.
  • Water recycling and conservation strategies.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Water Pollution & Conservation
summary_sections.content_html:
Water Pollution & Conservation

Types of Water Pollution

  • Point source: Identifiable origin (factory pipe, sewage outfall).
  • Non-point source: Diffuse origin (agricultural runoff, urban stormwater).
  • Pollutant types: Nutrients, pathogens, sediment, chemicals, thermal, plastic.

Conservation Strategies

  • Clean Water Act (1972 US), wastewater treatment, buffer zones, wetland protection.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Hydrology
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Hydrology

The Colorado River

Supplies 40 million people; over-allocated by 1922 Colorado River Compact; Lake Mead at historic lows.

Bangladesh Flooding

Extremely flood-prone: flat delta, monsoon rains, snowmelt from Himalayas, sea level rise. Affects millions annually.

Ogallala Aquifer Depletion

Supplies 30% of US irrigation water; being depleted 3-100× faster than recharge in many areas.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Ocean Circulation Models
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Ocean Circulation

Surface Circulation

Wind-driven gyres: North Atlantic, South Atlantic, North Pacific, South Pacific, Indian Ocean. Coriolis deflection + continental blocking.

Deep Circulation

Thermohaline conveyor belt: cold, salty water sinks in the North Atlantic → flows along the ocean bottom → upwells in Pacific/Indian → returns via surface currents. Full cycle takes ~1,000 years.

AP Calculations

Current velocity, Coriolis parameter, Ekman transport, heat transport calculations.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Environmental Engineering
